decision: Which hosting and deployment target category should be used to serve the application: a PaaS platform, a VPS, a container platform, or another managed runtime?

grounding.existing_system: n/a — greenfield (C3): no existing system to conform to
grounding.note: arch-canon.json absent; option set reasoned from established architecture first principles; currency self-verified against current platform offerings (2025); options must satisfy A6 (no single large-cloud-vendor lock-in), A13 (single-server), C2 (two-month timeline), C1 (browser-reachable); Kubernetes / auto-scaling container orchestration excluded as non-compliant with INV6

options.summary: Managed platform-as-a-service that abstracts server provisioning, OS management, and TLS termination; the team pushes code or a container image and the platform manages the runtime.
options.reason_to_consider: PaaS platforms (Render, Fly.io, Railway) provide the fastest path from code to a browser-reachable HTTPS URL, directly supporting AC1 and C2; they are available from multiple providers (satisfying A6) and have free or very low-cost tiers suitable for A13 personal-tool scale.

strengths: Fastest deployment path: automated TLS, HTTP routing, and environment-variable injection are provided; no server configuration required — strong alignment with C2 (two-month timeline) and AC1 (browser-reachable over HTTPS).
Multiple competing providers (Render, Fly.io, Railway, Heroku) are available; switching between them is feasible without code changes — satisfies A6 (no single-vendor lock-in).
Low operational overhead: no OS patching, no nginx configuration, no server provisioning — team focuses entirely on application code.
Managed add-on databases (Postgres) are available on all named platforms at low cost, compatible with DP4 relational options.
Suitable for A13 scale (single instance, synchronous requests, no horizontal scaling needed — INV6).
costs: PaaS pricing models change (Heroku's free tier removal is a historical example); migration away from a specific PaaS requires build-pipeline reconfiguration.

Some PaaS platforms (notably Render and Railway) do not support persistent-volume filesystem mounts on their basic tiers — relevant if SQLite (DP4 embedded option) is chosen; requires either a managed database add-on or a platform with volume support.
Less control over the runtime environment; if a specific system library is needed (e.g., for a PDF library dependency), the build image must be customizable.
Cold-start latency on platforms with sleep-on-idle behavior (Render free tier) can cause slow first-request responses; a paid plan eliminates this.

options.option: VPS with manual server setup (e.g., DigitalOcean Droplet, Hetzner Cloud, Linode/Akamai)
options.summary: A virtual private server where the team installs the runtime, configures a reverse proxy (nginx/Caddy), manages TLS, and deploys the application; full control over the OS and environment.
options.reason_to_consider: VPS hosting is the most portable, vendor-neutral option (A6) with no platform-specific deployment conventions; at A13 scale a small VPS (1–2 vCPU, 1–2 GB RAM) is sufficient and the monthly cost is predictable and low.

strengths: Maximum portability: VPS offerings are available from dozens of providers (DigitalOcean, Hetzner, Linode, OVH, Vultr) — no PaaS-specific build conventions, deepest A6 compliance.
Full OS-level control: any system library or runtime dependency can be installed; no PaaS build-image constraints.
Predictable, fixed monthly cost ($5–10/month for a small Hetzner or DigitalOcean droplet) — lowest cost option at A13 scale if the team is comfortable with server administration.
SQLite (if chosen in DP4) is fully compatible: the filesystem is persistent and directly under team control.
Single-server deployment model is a natural fit for INV6 (no horizontal scaling required).
costs: Highest operational overhead: the team must configure and maintain nginx/Caddy, TLS certificates (Let's Encrypt renewal), OS security updates, and process management (systemd, PM2, etc.) — consuming time from C2's two-month window.
No managed database included: a separate Postgres install on the same VPS or a managed database add-on (external) is required if relational DB is chosen.
Deployment automation (CI/CD pipeline, deploy scripts) must be built by the team; no platform-provided deploy hooks.
Server provisioning and initial configuration is a non-trivial setup step that does not exist with PaaS; mistakes can leave the server misconfigured for days.

options.option: Container platform — single-container deployment (e.g., Fly.io Machines, Render Docker, DigitalOcean App Platform with Docker)
options.summary: Application is packaged as a Docker container image and deployed to a single-container managed runtime; the container platform handles scheduling, TLS, and HTTP routing; no orchestration layer.
options.reason_to_consider: Containerizing the application decouples the runtime from the host OS (improving portability beyond a VPS without the PaaS constraint of a fixed buildpack), while keeping a single-instance deployment model consistent with INV6; multiple platforms support single-container Docker deployments at low cost.

strengths: Container image is portable across any Docker-compatible host — stronger portability than a PaaS-buildpack deployment; satisfies A6.
Deterministic runtime: the Dockerfile pins exact OS, runtime version, and system libraries, eliminating 'it works on my machine' build environment differences.
Single-container deployment remains compliant with INV6 (no orchestration, no horizontal scaling); the container is the single server process.
Platform-managed TLS and HTTP routing (Fly.io, Render Docker) provides the same operational simplicity as a PaaS for network-facing concerns.
Lower operational burden than a VPS: no OS-level maintenance, no nginx configuration.
costs: Dockerfile authoring and container build pipeline add an initial setup step not present in a pure PaaS buildpack deployment — consumes some C2 time.
Container image build times add to CI/CD latency; image caching must be configured to keep pipelines fast.
Persistent volume management for SQLite (if DP4 chooses embedded DB) requires explicit volume attachment in the container deployment configuration.
Container platform pricing varies; some platforms charge per compute-second (Fly.io Machines) vs. flat monthly (Render); cost unpredictability at low traffic is minor but real.
Team needs Docker familiarity; if the team is unfamiliar with containerization, this adds a learning curve under C2.

no_pick_note: Live, unranked alternatives. EVALUATE-DECIDE scores against CONSTRAINTS / ACCEPTANCE / NFRs and picks (role 4); OPTION-GEN does not recommend.
